preamble: The devoicing of voiced initial stops like [b] to [p] is a primary catalyst for Tonogenesis through 'Register Splits' or 'Tone Splits'. In this process, the historical voicing of the initial consonant is lost but preserved in the lower pitch (Low Register) of the following vowel, effectively doubling the number of tones in the language's inventory.
phoneticEffects: Devoicing, Register Split, Tonogenesis, Voice Loss

examples.note: Initial voicing loss triggered the development of the lower-register tones (Huyền, Nặng, etc.).
Voicing loss in initials led to the split of the original three tones into high and low registers.
Devoicing of the 'Muddy' (濁) initials created the Yīn (high) and Yáng (low) register distinction.

sources: Haudricourt, A. G. (1954). De l'origine des tons en viêtnamien.
Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
